Okay, so something extremely important that you need to pay attention to and need a concept you need to understand is the difference between an individual contributor and a leader. And so before you promote anyone, you must decide if they are a leader or an individual contributor. So all of us understand what a leader is because as a business owner you must be a leader. But you need to understand what an individual contributor is. And so why is this so necessary to understand? Because a mistake that a lot of us make is over promoting people. Okay, so what's over promoting? Over promoting is the result of a lack of objective insight combined with personal bias. Okay, most promotion decisions place more weight on how that person is doing in their current role rather than the measurement of their skills required to do the next role. Okay, but the truth is, is that oftentimes our highest performers who are the most productive are not the best candidates for managers. They're not the best candidates for leadership. Okay, often what gets a person to this level is not going to get them to this level. It's actually going to hinder them from getting to the next level. Okay, yet most companies are more likely to promote from the top performers than anyone else for a variety of reasons. Okay, so a lot of times it's because you want them to grow for growth's sake, not because they actually have been growing. You just see their high performance and sometimes high performance is seen as growth. But there's a difference between growth and high performance. Okay, in fact, high performance is usually because they are very competent and they're not being challenged and therefore they perform at a high level. Okay, two, you assume their skills will translate. You're like, well, they're so great at doing this, that must mean they're great at doing everything. No, a lot of times they're really great at doing that because they would be terrible at doing the next thing. Okay, and lastly, a lot of people feel really indebted, meaning that you feel like someone's been with you for a while, they put a ton of effort in, they really, really want it. Like they told you they want it, they told you they want to be a leader, but they're not. Okay, and so what is an individual contributor? Okay, they're the outliers on the team. They're those who are clearly the highest performers whose numbers consistently outdo the rest of the team. Often these people don't, however, have enough self-awareness to realize that they're best suited to just stay here. Okay, and their managers don't have yet enough awareness to put the best retention tools in place and incentives to keep them performing and fulfilled without moving them into a leadership position which will be their demise and probably the end of their career at your company. Okay, so the question is, how do we retain both? How are we going to retain people who might want to be in a leadership position but are not meant to be? Right? One, you need to be clear when you're promoting. Okay, so you need to identify areas of incompetence with a teammate. So if you are promoting someone, you need to be very, very clear about where you are worried and in that they don't have the skill fully yet or where you feel that they need to work on things in order to step into this new skill. You need that teammate to self-identify those things. Okay, it's not enough for you to tell someone where their deficiencies lie. They need to be able to repeat it back to you in full certainty. Okay? Third, you need to put a coaching plan in place. So are you going to have extra one-on-ones with them? Are they going to shadow you more? Are you going to have them shadow other people on the team? Are you going to have them complete a course or a certification? What is it that is going to be put in place so they can get better at that skill? Right? And then lastly is emotional capacity. Emotional capacity has to be identified. What is their emotional capacity? The higher that you go, the more emotional work is required and you have to look at yourself and look at that person and be able to really clearly see objectively. Do they have the emotional capacity to day after day deal with these people problems? Because as you get higher and as you're managing people, it's all people problems. It's not can you be a good trainer? Can you make a good program? Can you show up on time? Can you make people smile? It's can you deal with unemployment claims? Can you deal with people who don't show up? Can you deal with understanding how to influence and motivate? Can you deal with listening to someone's personal problems all day? There's a lot of stuff you have to deal with that has nothing to do with the last thing. You have to be very clear when you're promoting someone of exactly what that new role looks like. And then lastly, understanding the other ways to retain people. And so a lot of the times these are just as effective as promoting someone. One would be a lateral move. So a lot of times you don't just need to make an upward move, but it provides the same feeling of growth. So moving someone from say it's a trainer who's actually really good at sales, moving them sales or someone who's in sales, but really loves fulfilling and wowing clients, you move them to being a trainer. Those same moves can provide the exact same feeling of accomplishment as promoting someone. To investing in their skill growth. So if they want to be promoted, but you're not going to be able to because of these issues, then saying, okay, I'm going to invest in your ongoing growth because I need to add value to you. Maybe it's events, maybe it's conferences, maybe it's courses to take. Maybe you give them a goal. It's like if you reach X amount of, if you send out X amount of handwritten cards or if you make X amount of sales or if you retain X percentage of clients, then I'm going to send you this or I'm going to pay for the certification, right? Three is assigning stretch goals that cross departments. So giving someone the opportunity to attain those new skills by saying, hey, this person's out in town for a week. Like our operations manager is out in town for a week. So I wanted to ask if you wanted to step into that role for a week because I know you were interested. I know we didn't promote you, but I figured I'd give it a shot, right? Fourth adding ranks or merit badges. Okay. So instead of creating a new system, take the existing path or the existing infrastructure that you have, add new shorter titles, add competitions, add merit badges, add, you know, for a trainer, it might be like service to 1000 hours, you know, ran X amount of sessions. And once they reach that, suddenly they're a level two trainer or a tier three trainer. Okay. There's a lot of things you can do like that to incentivize people rather than promoting them. Okay. People like to do things that are interesting and entertaining. So like gamifying the experience for them is really exciting for people. And then lastly, it's like pairing them with another leader for mentorship or friendship. So maybe there's somebody else on the team that they need to have one-on-ones with that can provide them with something that you can't or it will give them an extra hour of coaching per week that they need and that, you know, they deserve as a member of your team.
